summary: Joel Spolsky argues that usability can be reduced to a single principle: something is usable if it behaves exactly as the user expects. He illustrates this through a detailed scenario of a Windows user named Pete struggling with a Mac, not because the Mac is poorly designed, but because it violates his ingrained expectations. The frustrations Pete experiences—window resizing, keyboard shortcuts, space bar behavior—are all trivial individually but compound into genuine unhappiness. Spolsky contends that debates about which OS has objectively better UI features miss the point entirely. What matters is whether the interface responds the way the user's mental model predicts it will. This principle, he argues, underpins virtually everything about usable design.
key_points: Usability boils down to one rule: something is usable if it behaves exactly as the user expected it to behave
A competent Windows user struggling with a Mac doesn't prove the Mac is poorly designed—it proves that violated expectations create frustration regardless of objective quality
Small interface frustrations compound into genuine unhappiness and a feeling of being out of control
OS flame wars about which UI features are objectively better miss the point—what matters is matching user expectations, not feature superiority
The choice between design conventions (e.g., resize from any edge vs. move from any edge) is largely arbitrary; consistency with user expectations is what determines perceived usability
money_quotes: Something is usable if it behaves exactly as expected.
Even if the whole thing is subconscious, the subtle feeling of being out of control translates into unhappiness.
All those flame wars you read on the OS bigot websites about user interface issues focus on the wrong thing.

The point is, does the UI respond to the user in the way in which the user expected it to respond? If it didn't, the user is going to feel like they can't control the interface, and they're going to be unsuccessful.
It's totally arbitrary which key you press to close a window.
Bad usability in the design of aircraft controls can result in what is cheerfully referred to as CFIT: Controlled Flight Into Terrain.
Making the users of your design unhappy is not likely to be precisely the result you were looking for, unless you're designing a French film.
key_insight: Usability is not about which design is objectively superior but whether the interface behaves the way the specific user expects it to, since violated expectations—no matter how trivial—compound into frustration and a feeling of lost control.
